query: is expert breast pathology assessment required for accurate diagnosis and management of ductal carcinoma in situ

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the epidemiology of ductal carcinoma in situ. Assess the evaluation of ductal carcinoma in situ. Evaluate the management options available for ductal carcinoma in situ. Communicate some interprofessional team strategies that can improve outcomes for patients who require ductal carcinoma in situ. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of the breast represents neoplastic lesions, typically localized to the breast ducts with no evidence of invasion into the surrounding tissue. The diagnosis of DCIS has significantly increased in the recent past since the implementation of routine screening mammograms. Therapeutic approaches include surgery, radiation therapy, and adjuvant endocrine therapy. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of DCIS and highlights the healthcare team's role in managing patients with DCIS.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Image-guided core needle biopsy performed on the area of suspicion provides a histologic diagnosis.  Core needle biopsy allows for more tissue to be sampled compared to fine-needle aspiration; this allows the pathologist to determine if breast cancer is invasive or noninvasive (DCIS). DCIS is architecturally classified as comedo, cribriform, micropapillary, papillary, or solid. Of note, even core needle biopsy has limitations. When core needle biopsy reveals DCIS, there is still a 10% to 20% chance that the final excisional surgical specimen contains invasive carcinoma. [12] Tissue biopsy should also be evaluated for hormone receptor status - particularly estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER-2) status. Although other receptors are being identified and studied, these 3 receptors are important to identify. Specific endocrine treatment modalities can be given to further reduce risk in patients with ER/PR/HER-2 receptor positivity.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), or intraductal carcinoma, is a non-invasive breast cancer characterized by a proliferation of abnormal epithelial cells confined within the basement membrane. Disruption of the basement membrane layer would change the diagnosis from DCIS to invasive breast cancer. DCIS is considered to be a precursor for invasive breast cancer. [1] Specifically, the World Health Organization defines the term DCIS as "a neoplastic proliferation of epithelial cells confined to the mammary ductal-lobular system and characterized by subtle to marked cytologic atypia and an inherent but not necessarily obligate tendency to progression to invasive breast cancer." [2] Although grouped, DCIS is, in reality, a heterogeneous group of lesions that varies in the clinical presentation, genetics, biomarkers, morphologic features, as well as the clinical potential to progress to invasive breast cancer. Rates of diagnosis have been increasing with the use of screening mammography that detects pre-clinical microcalcifications. However, the diagnosis of DCIS does require a tissue biopsy. The treatment for DCIS is multidisciplinary and may include surgery, hormone therapy, and radiation therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to be aware that if a patient has DCIS, there is a real risk the patient already has invasive breast cancer. Approximately 10% to 20% of DCIS lesions diagnosed on core needle biopsy be upgraded to invasive cancer after definitive resection; furthermore, large high-grade DCIS lesions have a higher likelihood of containing an invasive component. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: DCIS most commonly presents without a palpable mass and is commonly found via screening mammograms. Ninety percent of all cases of DCIS are detected from screening mammograms. [10] Additionally, if the patient was referred for a suspicious lesion during screening mammography, it is important to perform diagnostic mammography to characterize the ipsilateral disease and rule out bilateral disease. A screening mammogram consists of 2 views of the breast, and it is sensitive but not specific for breast malignancy. On mammograms, the presentation of clustered macrocalcification is common for DCIS. Calcifications associated with malignancy tend to be smaller (0.1 to 1 mm in diameter) and are usually clustered. Microcalcifications that appear in linear branching patterns or segmental types of pleomorphic calcifications are highly suspicious for DCIS. [11] Once suspicious calcifications are identified on screening mammography, it is important to proceed with diagnostic mammography, including additional cuts and information for surgical planning.

retrieved_docs.title: New Palpable Breast Mass -- Evaluation -- Pathology Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: Excisional biopsy was once the standard method for diagnosing palpable breast masses and suspicious nonpalpable lesions; however, core needle biopsy is now preferred as it is less damaging to surrounding breast tissue while providing accurate histologic diagnosis. An excisional breast biopsy is now primarily reserved for specific situations, including: Imaging and core biopsy results that are discordant Nondiagnostic core biopsy specimens Lesions inaccessible to core biopsy due to location or patient anatomy Findings of atypical hyperplasia or lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: DCIS treatment is multifaceted and should be tailored to the patient based on pathology, overall health, comorbidities, age, genetics, cosmetic concerns, hormone receptor status, medical access, social support, and patient preference. Therefore, it is paramount for the physician to have a careful discussion with the patient to make an informed decision regarding the treatment plan and ensure care coordination for each patient. These patients need coordination with some or all of the following specialties: General surgery/breast surgical oncology Radiology Medical genetics Plastic surgery Radiation oncology Internal medicine/medical oncology

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A recent meta-analysis from 2019 identified 6 factors that were linked to a higher risk of recurrence of invasive breast cancer. These factors are DCIS found by a doctor during a physical exam, premenopausal, positive margins, high-grade DCIS, high levels of p16 protein, and African American ethnicity. [28] While these results need to be validated by future studies, they highlight that DCIS is heterogeneous. In the future, differentiating between DCIS type and risk factors help guide prognosis and, eventually, treatment to avoid both under and overtreatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Care coordination utilizing case managers, social workers, and home health aides, which is crucial for improving access to care, can improve compliance with overall treatment plans in vulnerable populations.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Ductal Hyperplasia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: It is also crucial that surgeons know the appropriate surgical management of this finding. ADH on core needle biopsy will require needle or seed localized excisional biopsy to rule out nearby breast carcinoma. However, if ADH is found on excisional biopsy to be ADH only - even if margins are positive - the patient is surgically complete. [Level 2]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Ductal Carcinoma in Situ -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: With a positive diagnosis of DCIS, the patient needs to proceed to surgery for either breast conservation surgery or total mastectomy. Determination is made based on history and physical exam findings that are important to obtain during the initial evaluation. Cosmesis and the size of the lesion should be taken into consideration. Multifocal, multicentric disease and large lesion size may make breast conservation surgery a less cosmetic option than mastectomy. One must determine if the patient is suitable for breast conservation by evaluating if the patient has a contraindication to post-surgical radiation therapy, such as pregnancy or previous radiation therapy. Information regarding the patient's preferences and ability to follow-up for subsequent treatments with radiation therapy should also be elicited. Additionally, it should be determined on evaluation if the patient needs a referral for genetic counseling. This is determined by a careful personal and family history that uncovers higher than-expected breast and other cancer rates, particularly at young ages. These patients may decide to forgo breast conservation therapy for mastectomy if they discover they have a genetic predisposition to developing breast cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Early-Stage Breast Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for ductal carcinoma in situ (Stage 0) is individualized based on clinical and pathological features and patient preference. Typically, patients with ductal carcinoma in situ are treated with lumpectomy. Radiation therapy is recommended to reduce the risk of local breast recurrence. In some patients with a low-risk profile, radiation therapy can be omitted. Increasingly, Oncotype DX ductal carcinoma in situ score is utilized in the decision-making process for ductal carcinoma in situ to assess risk for recurrence and help guide treatment. In patients with invasive tumors, chemotherapy is recommended if there is a high risk of recurrence especially in the setting of a large tumor, node positive, triple-negative (ER, PR, HER2- negative), or HER2 positive disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Breast Hyperplasia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Atypical hyperplasia of the breast is defined as abnormal epithelial proliferative breast lesions that are not qualitatively or quantitatively abnormal enough to be classified as carcinoma in situ. Atypical hyperplasia is a premalignant condition and may occur in either ductal or lobular epithelium. This activity describes the evaluation, surveillance, and management of atypical breast hyperplasia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the care of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Tissue Evaluation for HER2 Tumor Marker -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Repeat testing is recommended if the initial test was HER2-positive in a histologic grade 1 carcinoma of infiltrating ductal or lobular carcinoma, hormone receptor-positive, and the HER2/chromosome 17 centromere (CEP17) ratio is <2 and an average HER2 copy number is between ≥4 and <6 signals/cell.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 897
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical site that undergoes both diagnostic and therapeutic procedures. Breast biopsy and other diagnostic interventions are performed on the female breast to obtain tissue samples and assess pathology. In addition, the female breast is the target of surgical treatments such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy, which are procedures used to remove localized breast lesions while preserving as much normal tissue as possible. These relationships underscore the continuum of care for breast conditions, linking diagnostic evaluation with appropriate surgical management.

retrieved_docs.content: The areola is identified as the direct site where a biopsy of a lesion is performed, and it is also recognized as a location where breast cancer may be found, highlighting its importance in diagnostic evaluation of potential breast malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: excision (lumpectomy/quadrantectomy) and total mastectomy with associated lymph‑node removal—providing clinicians with a structured framework for selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for breast pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
retrieved_docs.content: The procedures described as “Biopsy of lesion of areola” and “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” are both classified as breast biopsies (خزعة الثدي). These statements indicate that a biopsy targeting an areolar lesion and a biopsy that reveals a malignant breast neoplasm are each specific instances of the broader diagnostic technique of خزعة الثدي, which is used to obtain tissue samples from the breast for pathological evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: The statement “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” is identified as a خزعة الثدي (breast biopsy). This indicates that a tissue sample taken from the breast has revealed a malignant tumor, confirming the presence of breast cancer. Recognizing this finding as a type of breast biopsy is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of an appropriate treatment plan.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical site for a range of diagnostic and surgical interventions. Breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast are performed on the female breast to obtain tissue samples for evaluation, and this category specifically includes the procedure referred to in Arabic as خزعة الثدي. In addition, surgical treatments such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast are directly related to the female breast, aiming to excise localized lesions while preserving as much normal tissue as possible. Together, these diagnostic and therapeutic procedures constitute essential components of breast disease assessment and management, enabling accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 896
retrieved_docs.content: The observation that a biopsy of the breast shows a malignant neoplasm is a specific instance of سرطان الثدي (breast cancer). This relationship highlights that histopathologic confirmation of malignant cells in breast tissue directly defines the presence of breast cancer, which is critical for accurate diagnosis and subsequent treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of lesion of areola is a type of خزعة الثدي (breast biopsy) that is performed directly on the areola, making the areola the direct procedure site for this specific lesion biopsy. Similarly, the statement “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” also falls under the category of خزعة الثدي, indicating that a breast biopsy can reveal malignant pathology. Together, these concepts illustrate that breast biopsies (خزعة الثدي) may be targeted to distinct anatomical regions such as the areola and can provide critical diagnostic information when a malignant neoplasm is identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1976
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure termed Biopsy of lesion of areola is classified as a خزعة الثدي (breast biopsy) and is performed directly on the areola, which serves as the direct procedural site of the biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2207
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of lesion of areola is a specific form of خزعة الثدي (breast biopsy). The broader category described as breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on the breast includes خزعة الثدي as one of its constituent procedures. Consequently, خزعة الثدي represents a diagnostic intervention within the spectrum of breast procedures, and the areolar lesion biopsy is a particular application of this technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2176
retrieved_docs.content: The areola is anatomically classified as a part of the breast, representing a distinct breast component. When a lesion of the areola requires diagnostic evaluation, a biopsy of that lesion is performed directly on the areola, making the areola the specific procedural site for such biopsies. This relationship underscores the areola’s role both as a defined breast structure and as the direct target for tissue sampling procedures in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2079
retrieved_docs.content: "Breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast" is a broader category of which "خزعة الثدي" (breast biopsy) is a specific instance. The procedure "خزعة الثدي" is performed on partially excised breast anatomy, indicating that it is used to obtain tissue samples from breast tissue that has been partially removed. Additionally, the procedure "خزعة الثدي" is associated with the anatomic site of a malignant tumor in the nipple of females ("ورم خبيث في الحلمة لدى الإناث"), highlighting its role in diagnosing or evaluating such cancers. This interconnection underscores the importance of breast biopsy in the work‑up of nipple malignancies and in the management of partially excised breast tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2193
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” is a specific outcome of a breast biopsy (خزعة الثدي) and serves as a diagnostic indicator of breast cancer (سرطان الثدي). In other words, when a breast biopsy reveals a malignant neoplasm, it confirms the presence of breast cancer. Moreover, “breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast” (خزعة الثدي) represents the broader category of breast tissue sampling and related diagnostic techniques, of which the malignant‑neoplasm finding is one particular result. This relationship highlights how breast biopsy procedures are used to detect malignant disease and how the detection of a malignant neoplasm directly links the procedure to a diagnosis of breast cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1947
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of lesion of areola and biopsy of lesion of nipple are both classified as خزعة الثدي, indicating that these procedures are specific forms of breast biopsy that target lesions of the areola and nipple respectively. Recognizing these as subtypes of خزعة الثدي helps clinicians select appropriate diagnostic approaches for lesions located in these distinct anatomical regions of the breast.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: اللمفاوية الجذري) are specific modalities within the same therapeutic hierarchy. All mastectomy variants are members of both the malignant breast tumor SMQ and the set of therapeutic and diagnostic procedures for malignant diseases, and they are linked by the “completely_excised_anatomy_has_procedure” relationship to the anatomical structures they remove. Overall, the knowledge network connects diagnostic biopsies of the areola and nipple, the role of these breast parts as sites for cancer detection, and a hierarchical spectrum of surgical interventions—from targeted excisions of accessory or aberrant tissue to partial and total mastectomy with lymph‑node removal—under a unified framework of therapeutic procedures for malignant breast disease.
